examples.explanation: Step 1: Grid is [[0,2],[1,3]], max value = 3. Step 2: Binary search: left=0, right=3. Step 3: mid=1, BFS check level 1: Start at (0,0)=0≤1, neighbors: (0,1)=2>1 (blocked), (1,0)=1≤1 (visit), from (1,0): (1,1)=3>1 (blocked). No path to (1,1), so canSwim=false. Step 4: Update left=2. Step 5: mid=2, BFS check level 2: Start (0,0)=0≤2, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤2 (visit), (1,0)=1≤2 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=3>2 (blocked). From (1,0): (1,1)=3>2 (blocked). No path, canSwim=false. Step 6: Update left=3. Step 7: mid=3, BFS check level 3: Start (0,0)=0≤3, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤3 (visit), (1,0)=1≤3 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=3≤3 (visit, reached target). canSwim=true. Step 8: Update right=3. Step 9: left=3, right=3, loop ends. Return 3.

Step 1: Grid [[3,2],[0,1]], max=3. Step 2: left=0, right=3. Step 3: mid=1, BFS level 1: Start (0,0)=3>1 (blocked immediately, cannot even start). canSwim=false. Step 4: left=2. Step 5: mid=2, BFS level 2: Start (0,0)=3>2 (blocked). canSwim=false. Step 6: left=3. Step 7: mid=3, BFS level 3: Start (0,0)=3≤3, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤3 (visit), (1,0)=0≤3 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=1≤3 (visit, reached target). canSwim=true. Step 8: right=3. Step 9: left=3, right=3, return 3.

approaches.name: Brute Force with Binary Search O(n² log W)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on possible water levels from 0 to max grid value, and for each candidate level, perform DFS or BFS to check if a path exists from top-left to bottom-right where all cells have value ≤ level. This is a straightforward but slow method that explores all levels systematically.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n² log W) where n is grid dimension and W is max value
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for recursion stack or queue in BFS/DFS

ladders.title: Full Problem — Swim in Rising Water with Binary Search and BFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search on water levels with BFS path checking
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the swim in rising water problem by finding the minimum water level that allows swimming from top-left to bottom-right. The intuition is that if we can swim at level X, we can swim at any higher level, creating a monotonic property perfect for binary search. We search between 0 and the maximum grid value. For each candidate level, we perform BFS to check if a path exists where all cells have value ≤ level. The algorithm: 1) Find max grid value. 2) Binary search from 0 to max. 3) For each mid, BFS to check path existence. 4) Adjust search bounds based on result. Time complexity is O(n² log W) where n is grid dimension and W is max value, due to binary search (log W) and BFS (n²). Space is O(n²) for BFS queue and visited set. This connects to L1 by using the BFS path checker as a subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to include start cell in BFS check, incorrect binary search bounds adjustment, and not handling edge cases like 1x1 grid. Real code pattern: def swimInWater(grid): max_val = max(max(row) for row in grid); left, right = 0, max_val; while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if canSwim(grid, mid): right = mid; else: left = mid+1; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: Grid is [[0,2],[1,3]], max value = 3. Step 2: Binary search: left=0, right=3. Step 3: mid=1, BFS check level 1: Start at (0,0)=0≤1, neighbors: (0,1)=2>1 (blocked), (1,0)=1≤1 (visit), from (1,0): (1,1)=3>1 (blocked). No path to (1,1), so canSwim=false. Step 4: Update left=2. Step 5: mid=2, BFS check level 2: Start (0,0)=0≤2, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤2 (visit), (1,0)=1≤2 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=3>2 (blocked). From (1,0): (1,1)=3>2 (blocked). No path, canSwim=false. Step 6: Update left=3. Step 7: mid=3, BFS check level 3: Start (0,0)=0≤3, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤3 (visit), (1,0)=1≤3 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=3≤3 (visit, reached target). canSwim=true. Step 8: Update right=3. Step 9: left=3, right=3, loop ends. Return 3.

Step 1: Grid [[3,2],[0,1]], max=3. Step 2: left=0, right=3. Step 3: mid=1, BFS level 1: Start (0,0)=3>1 (blocked immediately, cannot even start). canSwim=false. Step 4: left=2. Step 5: mid=2, BFS level 2: Start (0,0)=3>2 (blocked). canSwim=false. Step 6: left=3. Step 7: mid=3, BFS level 3: Start (0,0)=3≤3, neighbors: (0,1)=2≤3 (visit), (1,0)=0≤3 (visit). From (0,1): (1,1)=1≤3 (visit, reached target). canSwim=true. Step 8: right=3. Step 9: left=3, right=3, return 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Path Check for Given Water Level
ladders.desc: Check if a path exists from top-left to bottom-right at a specific water level
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine determines whether you can swim from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1) when water rises to a given level. The intuition is that swimming is possible if there's a connected path where every cell's elevation is ≤ level. We use BFS to explore the grid starting from top-left, only moving to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) if their value ≤ level. If we reach bottom-right, return true; else false. Algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with (0,0) and visited set. 2) While queue not empty: pop cell, if it's target return true. 3) For each neighbor in bounds: if not visited and grid[neighbor] ≤ level, add to queue and visited. 4) Return false if queue empties. Time: O(n²) as we might visit all cells. Space: O(n²) for queue and visited. This feeds into L0 by being the checker in binary search. Common mistakes: not checking start cell against level, forgetting to mark visited when enqueuing (causing infinite loops), and incorrect neighbor directions. Real code pattern: def canSwim(grid, level): n=len(grid); if grid[0][0]>level: return False; queue=[(0,0)]; visited=set([(0,0)]); while queue: r,c=queue.pop(0); if r==n-1 and c==n-1: return True; for dr,dc in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: nr,nc=r+dr,c+dc; if 0<=nr<n and 0<=nc<n and (nr,nc) not in visited and grid[nr][nc]<=level: visited.add((nr,nc)); queue.append((nr,nc)); return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at (0,0)=0≤2, queue=[(0,0)], visited={(0,0)}. Step 2: Pop (0,0), not target. Neighbors: (0,1)=2≤2 (add to queue and visited), (1,0)=1≤2 (add). Queue=[(0,1),(1,0)], visited={(0,0),(0,1),(1,0)}. Step 3: Pop (0,1), not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2) out, (1,1)=3>2 (blocked), (-1,1) out. Step 4: Pop (1,0), not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0) out, (1,1)=3>2 (blocked), (1,-1) out. Step 5: Queue empty, return false.

Step 1: Start (0,0)=0≤3, queue=[(0,0)], visited={(0,0)}. Step 2: Pop (0,0), not target. Neighbors: (0,1)=2≤3 (add), (1,0)=1≤3 (add). Queue=[(0,1),(1,0)], visited={(0,0),(0,1),(1,0)}. Step 3: Pop (0,1), not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1)=3≤3 (add, target reached). Queue=[(1,0),(1,1)], visited updated. Step 4: Pop (1,0), not target. Neighbors include (1,1) visited. Step 5: Pop (1,1), it is target (r=1,c=1, n=2), return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Monotonic Function
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find minimum level where path exists
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the binary search aspect: finding the smallest water level that allows swimming. The intuition is that the function f(level) = canSwim(level) is monotonic—if true at level L, it's true for all levels > L. This monotonicity enables binary search over possible levels from 0 to max grid value. Algorithm: 1) Set left=0, right=max grid value. 2) While left < right: compute mid = (left+right)//2. 3) If canSwim(mid) is true, set right=mid (search lower half). 4) Else set left=mid+1 (search upper half). 5) Return left when loop ends. Time: O(log W) for binary search, where W is max value. Space: O(1) for pointers. This connects to L0 by providing the search framework that uses L1's checker. Common mistakes: incorrect loop condition (use < not <= to avoid infinite loops), wrong update rules (right=mid when true, left=mid+1 when false), and off-by-one errors in bounds. Real code pattern: left, right = 0, max_val; while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if canSwim(grid, mid): right = mid; else: left = mid+1; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3. Step 2: mid=1, canSwim(1)=false, so left=2. Step 3: left=2, right=3, mid=2, canSwim(2)=false, so left=3. Step 4: left=3, right=3, loop ends (left<right false). Return left=3.
Step 1: left=0, right=5. Step 2: mid=2, canSwim(2)=true, so right=2. Step 3: left=0, right=2, mid=1, canSwim(1)=false, so left=2. Step 4: left=2, right=2, loop ends. Return left=2.

testCases.input: max_val=7, canSwim results: all false except level 7=true
max_val=4, canSwim results: all true from level 0

testCases.expected: 3 (but note: in full problem, this case wouldn't occur as start cell ≤ max)

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with BFS
ladders.desc: Traverse a grid using BFS to explore connected cells
ladders.explanation: This level covers BFS traversal on a 2D grid, a fundamental technique for exploring cells layer by layer. The intuition is to simulate water flow or movement by visiting cells starting from a source, expanding to neighbors systematically. Algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with start cell and visited set. 2) While queue not empty: pop cell, process it (e.g., check if target). 3) For each neighbor (up, down, left, right) within grid bounds: if not visited and meets condition (e.g., value ≤ threshold), add to queue and visited. Time: O(n²) worst-case. Space: O(n²) for queue and visited. This connects to L1 as the core of path checking. Common mistakes: using DFS instead of BFS (works but BFS is more intuitive for shortest path), incorrect neighbor coordinates, not marking visited when enqueuing (causes repeats), and using list as queue inefficiently (use collections.deque). Real code pattern: from collections import deque; def bfs(grid, start): n=len(grid); queue=deque([start]); visited=set([start]); while queue: r,c=queue.popleft(); # process cell; for dr,dc in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: nr,nc=r+dr,c+dc; if 0<=nr<n and 0<=nc<n and (nr,nc) not in visited: visited.add((nr,nc)); queue.append((nr,nc)).

examples.input: Grid: 2x2 all zeros, start=(0,0), condition: always true
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[(0,0)], visited={(0,0)}. Step 2: pop (0,0), neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0) in bounds, not visited, add both. queue=[(0,1),(1,0)], visited={(0,0),(0,1),(1,0)}. Step 3: pop (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2) out, (1,1) not visited add, (-1,1) out. queue=[(1,0),(1,1)], visited updated. Step 4: pop (1,0), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0) out, (1,1) visited, (1,-1) out. Step 5: pop (1,1), neighbors: (0,1) visited, (2,1) out, (1,0) visited, (1,2) out. Queue empty, visited all 4 cells.

Step 1: queue=[(0,0)], visited={(0,0)}, grid[0][0]=1≤2. Step 2: pop (0,0), neighbors: (0,1)=2≤2 (add), (1,0)=3>2 (blocked). queue=[(0,1)], visited={(0,0),(0,1)}. Step 3: pop (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1)=4>2 (blocked), others out. Queue empty, visited only cells with value ≤2.

testCases.expected: visited: all cells except maybe if values >1, but here all ≤1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Cell Accessibility in Grid
ladders.desc: Determine if a cell can be moved to based on a condition
ladders.explanation: This level involves checking whether you can move from one cell to an adjacent cell in a grid, given a condition like water level. The intuition is that swimming or movement is only possible if the target cell's elevation is ≤ water level. Operation: given current cell (r,c), candidate neighbor (nr,nc), grid, and level, return true if: 1) (nr,nc) is within grid bounds (0≤nr<n, 0≤nc<n). 2) grid[nr][nc] ≤ level. This is a fundamental predicate used in BFS traversal. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L3 as the condition check during BFS. Common mistakes: forgetting bounds check (leading to index errors), using strict inequality incorrectly (should be ≤ not <), and not comparing the correct cell value. Real code pattern: def canMove(grid, r, c, nr, nc, level): n=len(grid); if not (0<=nr<n and 0<=nc<n): return False; return grid[nr][nc] <= level.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check bounds: nr=0,nc=1, n=2, 0≤0<2 and 0≤1<2 true. Step 2: grid[0][1]=2, level=2, 2≤2 true. Return true.
Step 1: Bounds: nr=1,nc=1, 0≤1<2 true. Step 2: grid[1][1]=3, level=2, 3≤2 false. Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Compare Numbers with Threshold
ladders.desc: Check if a number is less than or equal to a given value
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: comparing a cell's elevation to a water level to determine if it's swimmable. The intuition is that water covers a cell if the water level is at least as high as the cell's elevation. Operation: given a cell value v and level L, return true if v ≤ L, else false. This models whether a cell is accessible when water rises to level L. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L4 as the core comparison in cell accessibility. Common mistakes: using < instead of ≤ (water level equal to elevation means covered), confusing variables, or incorrect data types. Real code pattern: def isSwimmable(v, L): return v <= L.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 3 and 5. Step 2: 3 ≤ 5 is true. Return true.
Step 1: Compare 7 and 4. Step 2: 7 ≤ 4 is false. Return false.

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Priority Queue O(n² log n)
approaches.summary: Treat the grid as a graph where edge weight is the max of current and neighbor cell values, and use Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap to find the path minimizing the maximum water level encountered. This efficiently finds the minimax path by always expanding the lowest possible water level.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for heap and visited set

ladders.title: Full Problem — Swim in Rising Water with Dijkstra's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Dijkstra with priority queue to find minimax path through grid
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding a path from top-left to bottom-right in an n×n grid where each cell has an elevation representing water level. The goal is to minimize the maximum water level encountered along any path. We model this as a graph problem where each cell is a node, and edges connect adjacent cells (up, down, left, right). The weight of moving from cell A to cell B is max(elevation[A], elevation[B]) - representing the water level needed to swim through both cells. Using Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap allows us to efficiently find the path that minimizes this maximum value. The algorithm maintains a priority queue of (current_max_level, row, col) tuples, always expanding the node with the smallest maximum level encountered so far. We track visited cells to avoid cycles. The time complexity is O(n² log n) because each of the n² cells can be pushed/popped from the heap with O(log n) operations. Space complexity is O(n²) for the heap and visited set. The key insight is that Dijkstra's algorithm, typically used for minimizing sum of weights, also works for minimizing maximum weight when we redefine edge weights appropriately. This is a classic minimax path problem where we want to minimize the maximum obstacle (water level) rather than the total distance.

examples.trace: Grid: [[0,2],[1,3]]. Start at (0,0) with max_level=0. Heap: [(0,0,0)]. Pop (0,0,0). Visit neighbors: (0,1) max=max(0,2)=2, push (2,0,1); (1,0) max=max(0,1)=1, push (1,1,0). Heap: [(1,1,0),(2,0,1)]. Pop (1,1,0). Visit neighbors: (1,1) max=max(1,3)=3, push (3,1,1). Heap: [(2,0,1),(3,1,1)]. Pop (2,0,1). Visit neighbors: (1,1) already in heap with level 3 (better than max(2,3)=3). Heap: [(3,1,1)]. Pop (3,1,1) - destination reached. Return 3.

Large 5×5 grid. Start at (0,0) level=0. Dijkstra expands: (0,0)→(0,1) max=1→(0,2) max=2→(0,3) max=3→(0,4) max=4→(1,4) max=5→(2,4) max=16 (jump from 5 to 16). Continue exploring but 16 remains minimum max to reach (4,4). Final path maximum is 16.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Custom Edge Weights
ladders.desc: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm with priority queue for grid graph with max-based edge weights
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core Dijkstra algorithm adapted for our minimax problem. Instead of summing edge weights, we track the maximum value encountered along the path. The algorithm starts from the source cell (0,0) with initial max_level = grid[0][0]. We use a min-heap (priority queue) that stores tuples of (current_max_level, row, col). At each step, we pop the node with the smallest current_max_level. For each unvisited neighbor (up, down, left, right), we calculate new_max = max(current_max_level, neighbor_value). If this new_max is better (smaller) than any previously recorded value for that neighbor, we push (new_max, neighbor_row, neighbor_col) to the heap. We continue until we reach the destination (n-1, n-1). The algorithm guarantees we find the minimum maximum value because it always expands the most promising path first. Time complexity: O(V log V) where V=n². Space: O(V) for heap and visited tracking. This sub-routine is the engine of the full solution - it takes the grid and returns the minimax value. The adaptation from standard Dijkstra is crucial: standard Dijkstra minimizes sum of weights, but here we minimize maximum weight by redefining relaxation.

examples.trace: Initialize heap with (0,0,0). Visited set empty. Step1: pop (0,0,0), mark visited. Neighbors: (0,1) new_max=max(0,2)=2, push (2,0,1); (1,0) new_max=max(0,1)=1, push (1,1,0). Step2: pop (1,1,0), mark visited. Neighbors: (1,1) new_max=max(1,3)=3, push (3,1,1). Step3: pop (2,0,1), mark visited. Neighbors: (1,1) already has level 3. Step4: pop (3,1,1) - destination reached. Return 3.

Start: heap [(1,0,0)]. Pop (1,0,0). Neighbors: (0,1) new_max=max(1,3)=3, push (3,0,1); (1,0) new_max=max(1,2)=2, push (2,1,0). Heap: [(2,1,0),(3,0,1)]. Pop (2,1,0). Neighbors: (1,1) new_max=max(2,0)=2, push (2,1,1). Pop (2,1,1) - destination. Return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimax Path Finding via Maximum Edge Weight
ladders.desc: Transform minimax problem into graph shortest path by defining edge weight as max of two cells
ladders.explanation: The central insight is converting the minimax problem into a standard graph path problem. In the original problem, we want to minimize the maximum water level along a path. We can think of moving from cell A to cell B as requiring water level of at least max(value[A], value[B]) - you need enough water to cover the higher of the two cells. This transforms each edge's weight from a distance into a 'requirement level'. The path's total requirement is the maximum of all edge weights along that path (not the sum). Dijkstra's algorithm can minimize maximum weight if we: 1) Store current maximum instead of cumulative sum, 2) Compare new paths based on their maximum weight, 3) Always expand the path with smallest current maximum. This works because if we have two paths to the same cell, the one with smaller maximum is always better for reaching the destination - any continuation from that cell will have maximum at least as large. The relaxation condition becomes: if new_max < recorded_max for a cell, update it. This is different from standard Dijkstra where we check if new_distance < old_distance. Time complexity remains O(E log V) where E≈4n². This core logic enables using well-known shortest path algorithms for minimax problems.

examples.trace: Consider 4 cells in line: [0,1,2,3]. Path1: go directly 0→2 (max(0,2)=2), then 2→3 (max(2,3)=3), overall max=3. Path2: 0→1 (max=1), 1→3 (max(1,3)=3), overall max=3. Path3: 0→1 (max=1), 1→2 (max(1,2)=2), 2→3 (max(2,3)=3), but careful - the maximum along path is 2 (from edge 1→2), not 3, because we track maximum as we go: after 0→1→2, max=2; then 2→3 doesn't increase max since 2>3? Wait, max(2,3)=3, so actually max becomes 3. Let me correct: For path 0→1→2→3: Step1: 0→1 max=1, Step2: 1→2 max=max(1,2)=2, Step3: 2→3 max=max(2,3)=3. So max=3. Actually all paths give 3. Better example: cells [0,5,1,4]. Path1: 0→5→4: edges: max(0,5)=5, max(5,4)=5, overall max=5. Path2: 0→1→4: max(0,1)=1, max(1,4)=4, overall max=4. So path2 is better with max=4.

Two possible paths: right then down: a→b→d. Edge weights: max(a,b) and max(b,d). Path maximum = max(max(a,b), max(b,d)) = max(a,b,d). Down then right: a→c→d. Maximum = max(a,c,d). The answer is minimum of these two maxima. This shows how minimax works.

ladders.title: Building Block — Priority Queue (Min-Heap) for Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement and use min-heap to always expand the most promising node first
ladders.explanation: A priority queue (specifically a min-heap) is essential for Dijkstra's efficiency. Instead of checking all nodes each iteration (O(n²) per step), the heap lets us retrieve the node with smallest current maximum in O(log n) time. We store tuples (max_level, row, col) where max_level is the priority. The heap always returns the smallest max_level. When we find a better path to a node (with smaller max_level), we push the new tuple into the heap. Note: we don't update existing entries; we allow duplicates and use a visited set or distance array to ignore outdated entries. This is standard Dijkstra optimization. Implementation details: In Python, use heapq module with heapq.heappush() and heapq.heappop(). Initialize with (grid[0][0], 0, 0). While heap not empty: pop smallest, if visited continue, if destination return max_level, else mark visited and process neighbors. For each neighbor in bounds: new_max = max(current_max, neighbor_value). If new_max < best_known[neighbor], update best_known and push to heap. Time: each node processed once, each edge considered once, heap operations O(log V). Space: O(V) for heap and visited/best_known. This building block is crucial because without the heap, algorithm becomes O(n⁴). The heap ensures we explore promising paths first.

examples.input: Heap operations: start with [(3,0,0)], add (5,0,1) and (4,1,0)
examples.trace: Initial heap: [(3,0,0)]. Push (5,0,1): heap becomes [(3,0,0),(5,0,1)]. Push (4,1,0): heap becomes [(3,0,0),(5,0,1),(4,1,0)] (min-heap property). Pop: gets (3,0,0). Now heap: [(4,1,0),(5,0,1)]. Pop: gets (4,1,0). Heap: [(5,0,1)]. Pop: gets (5,0,1). This shows smallest values processed first.

examples.input: Updating paths: node A has entry (7,A) in heap, we find better path with max=5
examples.output: Push (5,A) to heap, ignore old (7,A) when popped
examples.trace: Suppose node A initially reached with max=7, so heap has (7,A). Later we find path to A with max=5. We push (5,A) to heap. Now heap has both (5,A) and (7,A). When we pop, we get (5,A) first. We process A and mark it visited. When (7,A) pops later, we check visited and skip it. This avoids expensive heap deletion.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Neighbor Traversal and Boundary Checking
ladders.desc: Iterate through adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) with bounds checking
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing a cell's four neighbors in a 2D grid. Given a cell at (row, col), its neighbors are: (row-1, col) up, (row+1, col) down, (row, col-1) left, (row, col+1) right. We must check that each neighbor is within grid bounds: 0 ≤ row < n and 0 ≤ col < n. This is typically done with direction arrays: dirs = [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]. For each (dr,dc) in dirs, new_r = r+dr, new_c = c+dc. If 0≤new_r<n and 0≤new_c<n, then (new_r,new_c) is a valid neighbor. This operation is performed repeatedly in Dijkstra's algorithm when processing each node. Time: O(1) per neighbor, O(4) per node. Space: O(1) for direction arrays. Proper bounds checking prevents index errors. This seems simple but is critical: missing bounds checks cause crashes; incorrect neighbor definitions (including diagonals) give wrong results. In the swim problem, we only move to adjacent cells (not diagonals) because swimming through water assumes direct adjacency. This operation feeds into the building block by providing the edges to consider from each node.

examples.trace: For cell at row=1, col=1 in 3×3 grid (n=3). Directions: up: (1-1,1)=(0,1) valid; down: (1+1,1)=(2,1) valid; left: (1,1-1)=(1,0) valid; right: (1,1+1)=(1,2) valid. All four neighbors are within 0..2 range.
Cell (0,0). Directions: up: (-1,0) invalid (row<0); down: (1,0) valid; left: (0,-1) invalid (col<0); right: (0,1) valid. So only two neighbors.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Grids and Adjacency
ladders.desc: Represent 2D grid as graph with cells as nodes and adjacent cells as edges
ladders.explanation: A 2D grid is a matrix of rows and columns. Each cell can be represented as a node in a graph. Edges connect adjacent cells (neighbors). In a grid graph, each cell (except boundaries) has up to 4 neighbors: up, down, left, right. This is called 4-directional adjacency. We can think of the grid as a graph where nodes are cells and edges exist between adjacent cells. The grid values become node weights or edge weights depending on problem. For example, in a 3×3 grid, cell (1,1) (center) connects to (0,1), (2,1), (1,0), (1,2). Cell (0,0) (corner) connects only to (0,1) and (1,0). This foundation is essential because many problems (like this swim problem) use grid graphs. Understanding how to navigate grids using row and column indices is prerequisite. We access grid[r][c] where r is row index (0 to n-1), c is column index (0 to n-1). Moving up decreases r by 1, moving down increases r by 1, moving left decreases c by 1, moving right increases c by 1. Always check bounds to stay within grid. This concept feeds into basic operation of neighbor traversal.

examples.input: 2×2 grid with cells labeled A(0,0), B(0,1), C(1,0), D(1,1)
examples.output: Graph edges: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D
examples.trace: Cell A at (0,0): neighbors are B (right: (0,1)) and C (down: (1,0)). So edges A-B and A-C. Cell B at (0,1): neighbors A (left) and D (down: (1,1)). Edges B-A (already counted) and B-D. Cell C at (1,0): neighbors A (up) and D (right). Edges C-A (counted) and C-D. Cell D at (1,1): neighbors B (up) and C (left). Edges D-B and D-C (both counted). Total edges: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D.

examples.input: 3×1 grid (column) with cells X(0,0), Y(1,0), Z(2,0)
examples.trace: Only vertical adjacency. X neighbors: Y (down). Y neighbors: X (up) and Z (down). Z neighbors: Y (up). So edges: X-Y and Y-Z.

approaches.name: Union-Find with Sorted Cells O(n² log n)
approaches.summary: Sort all cells by their elevation values, then iterate through them in increasing order, using union-find to connect adjacent cells as they become accessible. Stop when the top-left and bottom-right cells are connected, and the current elevation is the answer. This leverages incremental connectivity without pathfinding.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n² log n) due to sorting

ladders.title: Full Problem — Swim in Rising Water with Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using union-find with sorted cells to find the minimum maximum elevation path.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the minimum time (elevation) needed to swim from top-left to bottom-right in a grid where water rises uniformly. The key insight is that we can think of this as finding the minimum maximum elevation along any path. Instead of exploring paths directly, we can use a union-find data structure to incrementally connect cells as they become accessible when water rises. We sort all cells by their elevation values, then process them from lowest to highest. For each cell, we mark it as accessible and union it with any adjacent accessible cells. We stop when the top-left (0,0) and bottom-right (n-1,n-1) cells become connected in the union-find structure. The current elevation at that moment is our answer. This works because we're essentially simulating water rising and checking connectivity at each elevation level. The union-find allows efficient connectivity checks without recomputing paths. Time complexity is O(n² log n) due to sorting n² cells, plus O(n² α(n)) for union operations (where α is inverse Ackermann, effectively constant). Space is O(n²) for union-find arrays and sorted list.

examples.trace: Grid: [[0,2],[1,3]]. Step 1: Sort cells by elevation: (0,0)=0, (1,0)=1, (0,1)=2, (1,1)=3. Step 2: Process elevation 0 at (0,0): union with neighbors (none accessible). Check connectivity: (0,0) not connected to (1,1). Step 3: Process elevation 1 at (1,0): union with neighbor (0,0) accessible. Now (0,0)-(1,0) connected. Check connectivity: (0,0) not connected to (1,1). Step 4: Process elevation 2 at (0,1): union with neighbor (0,0) accessible. Now (0,0)-(1,0)-(0,1) connected. Check connectivity: (0,0) not connected to (1,1). Step 5: Process elevation 3 at (1,1): union with neighbors (1,0) and (0,1) both accessible. Now all cells connected. Check connectivity: (0,0) connected to (1,1). Answer is current elevation 3.

5x5 grid with spiral pattern. Step 1: Sort all 25 cells by elevation. Step 2: Process cells from elevation 0 upward. Elevations 0-15: Connect various cells but (0,0) not connected to (4,4). At elevation 16: Cell (2,4) with value 16 becomes accessible. It connects to (2,3)=15 and (3,4)=20 (not accessible yet). The connection through (2,3) links to left side. Check connectivity: (0,0) now connected to (4,4) through path. Answer is 16.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Connectivity Check with Sorted Processing
ladders.desc: Process sorted cells and union adjacent accessible cells until start and end connect.
ladders.explanation: This is the core routine that implements the union-find approach. We take a sorted list of cells (by elevation) and process them in order. For each cell, we mark it as 'accessible' (typically by adding it to a visited set or union-find structure). Then we check its four neighbors (up, down, left, right). If a neighbor is also accessible, we perform a union operation to connect the current cell with that neighbor. After processing each cell, we check if the start cell (0,0) and end cell (n-1,n-1) are in the same union-find component. If they are, we return the current cell's elevation as the answer. The intuition is that we're simulating water rising: as water reaches a certain elevation, all cells at or below that elevation become accessible. By connecting adjacent accessible cells, we build up connectivity regions. When start and end finally connect, the water has risen just enough to create a path between them. The minimum elevation at which this happens is our answer. Time complexity for this routine is O(n² α(n)) where α is inverse Ackermann (effectively constant), plus O(n²) for neighbor checks. Space is O(n²) for union-find and visited tracking.

examples.trace: Initialize union-find for 4 cells. Step 1: Process (0,0,0): Mark accessible. No neighbors accessible. Check connectivity: find(0)≠find(3). Step 2: Process (1,0,1): Mark accessible. Check neighbor (0,0) accessible → union(1,0). Check connectivity: find(0)≠find(3). Step 3: Process (0,1,2): Mark accessible. Check neighbor (0,0) accessible → union(2,0). Check connectivity: find(0)≠find(3). Step 4: Process (1,1,3): Mark accessible. Check neighbors: (1,0) accessible → union(3,1); (0,1) accessible → union(3,2). Now all cells connected. Check connectivity: find(0)=find(3). Return elevation 3.

Initialize union-find. Step 1: Process (0,1,3): Mark accessible. No neighbors. Check: find(0)≠find(3). Step 2: Process (1,0,4): Mark accessible. No neighbors. Check: find(0)≠find(3). Step 3: Process (0,0,5): Mark accessible. Check neighbor (0,1) accessible → union(0,1). Check: find(0)≠find(3). Step 4: Process (1,1,6): Mark accessible. Check neighbor (1,0) accessible → union(3,2). Now (0,0)-(0,1) connected, (1,0)-(1,1) connected but these groups not connected. Check: find(0)≠find(3). Wait need more cells? Actually all cells processed but start and end not connected. This shows need for proper sorting - should include all cells. In full problem, we'd continue until connection.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Incremental Connectivity with Union-Find
ladders.desc: Using union-find to maintain connectivity as cells become accessible.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is maintaining dynamic connectivity using union-find (disjoint set union). As we process cells in increasing elevation order, we need to know when the start and end cells become connected through accessible cells. Union-find provides efficient O(α(n)) operations for union and find. Each cell is represented as a node in the union-find structure. Initially, all cells are in separate sets. When a cell becomes accessible (water reaches its elevation), we union it with any adjacent accessible cells. This gradually builds connected components of accessible cells. The key insight is that we don't need to store paths or distances - we only need to know if there's any connection between start and end. Union-find gives us this information efficiently. The 'incremental' aspect is crucial: we add cells one by one (in sorted order) and update connectivity incrementally. This avoids recomputing connectivity from scratch each time. The algorithm stops at the minimum elevation where find(start) == find(end). This works because if they connect at elevation E, any path between them must have maximum elevation at least E (since we haven't processed higher elevations yet), and E is sufficient (since all cells ≤ E are accessible). Time complexity for union-find operations is O(n² α(n)) which is effectively O(n²). Space is O(n²) for parent and rank arrays.

examples.trace: Step 1: Add (0,0): sets {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}. Start=0, end=3 not connected. Step 2: Add (1,0): union with (0,0) → sets {0,1}, {2}, {3}. Not connected. Step 3: Add (0,1): union with (0,0) → sets {0,1,2}, {3}. Not connected. Step 4: Add (1,1): union with (1,0) and (0,1) → all in set {0,1,2,3}. Now connected.

Imagine 3x3 grid where start=(0,0), end=(2,2). Process cells: First row (0,0),(0,1),(0,2) become accessible and connected. Then column (1,0),(2,0) become accessible and connect to (0,0). But start and end not connected yet. When cell (1,1) becomes accessible, it connects row and column components, making start and end connected.

ladders.title: Building Block — Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) Data Structure
ladders.desc: Implement union-find with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: Union-find is a data structure that maintains a collection of disjoint sets and supports two operations: find(x) returns the representative of the set containing x, and union(x,y) merges the sets containing x and y. For efficiency, we use two optimizations: path compression (during find, make nodes point directly to root) and union by rank (attach smaller tree to larger tree). We represent each element as a node with a parent pointer. Initially, each element is its own parent (self-root). The find operation follows parent pointers to the root, compressing paths along the way. The union operation finds roots of both elements and attaches one root to the other based on rank (tree height). Rank helps keep trees flat. With these optimizations, both operations take O(α(n)) time amortized, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (extremely slow-growing, ≤ 4 for practical n). This is effectively constant time. For the swim problem, we have n² elements (grid cells). We map each cell (i,j) to an index k = i*n + j. The union-find structure needs parent array of size n² and rank array of size n². When we union adjacent cells, we're connecting regions of accessible cells. The key is that union-find gives us efficient connectivity queries without storing explicit graph structures.

examples.input: Elements: 0,1,2,3. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2), find(0), find(3)
examples.trace: Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Step1: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1. rank0=0, rank1=0 → attach 1 to 0. parent=[0,0,2,3], rank=[1,0,0,0]. Step2: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3. rank2=0, rank3=0 → attach 3 to 2. parent=[0,0,2,2], rank=[1,0,1,0]. Step3: union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2. rank0=1, rank2=1 → attach 2 to 0. parent=[0,0,0,2], rank=[2,0,1,0]. Now find(0)=0, find(3): 3→2→0, so same set.

examples.input: Elements: 0,1,2,3,4. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(4), union(1,4), find(2), find(4)
examples.trace: Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): attach 1 to 0 → parent=[0,0,2,3,4], rank=[1,0,0,0,0]. union(2,3): attach 3 to 2 → parent=[0,0,2,2,4], rank=[1,0,1,0,0]. find(0)=0, find(4)=4. union(1,4): find(1)=0, find(4)=4 → attach 4 to 0 → parent=[0,0,2,2,0], rank=[2,0,1,0,0]. find(2)=2, find(4)=0 → different sets.

testCases.input: Elements: 5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2), check 0-3
Elements: 6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,2), union(3,4), check 0-5
Elements: 4, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), check 0-3
Elements: 8, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3), union(5,7), check 0-6
Elements: 8, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3), union(3,5), union(5,7), check 0-6
Elements: 10, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(8,9), union(1,3), union(3,5), union(5,7), union(7,9), check 0-9

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sorting Cells by Elevation
ladders.desc: Create and sort list of all grid cells with their elevation values.
ladders.explanation: The first step in the algorithm is to collect all cells of the grid along with their elevation values, then sort them in ascending order. Each cell is represented as a tuple (elevation, row, column) or (row, column, elevation). We need to sort primarily by elevation, but including coordinates helps identify the cell later. For an n×n grid, there are n² cells. We iterate through all rows and columns, adding each cell to a list. Then we sort this list using the elevation as key. Sorting n² elements takes O(n² log n²) = O(n² log n) time. This is the dominant time complexity of the overall algorithm. The intuition is that we need to process cells in order of increasing water level. By sorting, we ensure we always process the lowest unprocessed elevation next. This guarantees that when we check connectivity at elevation E, all cells with elevation ≤ E have been processed and marked accessible. An alternative would be to use a priority queue (heap) and process cells incrementally, but sorting is simpler here since we need all cells eventually. After sorting, we iterate through the list linearly. Space needed is O(n²) for the sorted list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Grid Representation and Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Represent 2D grid and access adjacent cells (up, down, left, right).
ladders.explanation: The foundation concept is representing a 2D grid in code and accessing a cell's neighbors. A grid is typically represented as a 2D list (list of lists) in Python, where grid[i][j] gives the value at row i, column j. Rows and columns are usually 0-indexed. For an n×n grid, valid indices are 0 to n-1 for both rows and columns. To access neighbors of a cell (i,j), we consider four directions: up (i-1,j), down (i+1,j), left (i,j-1), right (i,j+1). We must check that neighbor indices are within bounds (0 ≤ neighbor row < n and 0 ≤ neighbor column < n) before accessing. This avoids index errors. In the swim problem, we need to check neighbors to union adjacent accessible cells. The pattern is to define a list of direction vectors: directions = [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)] representing (delta_row, delta_column). Then for each direction, compute new coordinates and check bounds. This is a fundamental pattern in grid-based algorithms. Understanding this is prerequisite for implementing the neighbor checks in union-find. Time complexity for checking all four neighbors is O(1) per cell. Space is O(1) for direction vectors.

examples.trace: Cell at row 1, column 1. Check up: (1-1,1) = (0,1) within 0..2. Check down: (1+1,1) = (2,1) within bounds. Check left: (1,1-1) = (1,0) within bounds. Check right: (1,1+1) = (1,2) within bounds. All four neighbors valid.
Cell (0,0). Check up: (-1,0) invalid (row < 0). Check down: (1,0) valid. Check left: (0,-1) invalid (col < 0). Check right: (0,1) valid. So two valid neighbors.
